analysis.summary: First Mode 5 audit. No discrepancies between dome site state and our predictions.json. Poller confirms 10th consecutive quiet poll — no PROS items added, promoted, suspended, or removed since last tracked changes (2026-03-15 batch). All 8 PROS entries have detail_reasoning but none have tldr fields.
analysis.headline_finding: The dome's prospective bucket claims an apparent 88% hit rate (7/8 promoted), but this collapses to 50% (1/2) when restricted to genuinely prospective items where data did not predate registration. The one genuine success (PROS-007, field decay >=28 nT) is standard IGRF secular variation — our verdict is standard_physics. The one genuine failure (PROS-003, eclipse magnetic geometry) was silently suspended alongside WIN-010/WIN-025 in the 2026-03-15 audit.

genuine_vs_retrodictive.note: 6 of 8 PROS items had data_predates_registration=true. By the dome author's own framework ("What Prospective Means" box), these have lower evidential weight. Our analysis shows them as standard_physics retrodictions relabeled as prospective — the dome adopted known observations as predictions after the data was published.

analysis.tldr_recommendation: All 8 PROS entries lack tldr fields. The build renders TLDRs in collapsed summary bars. Recommend backfilling at minimum the 2 genuinely prospective entries (PROS-003, PROS-007) since those are the most frequently cited in the prospective hit-rate narrative.

metrics.note: The 88% apparent hit rate is inflated by including 6 retrodictions where data predated registration. The genuine prospective hit rate of 50% is the epistemologically meaningful figure. Even the single genuine "hit" (PROS-007) is explained by standard secular variation models.

action_for_decider: No immediate changes needed. Recommend: (1) backfill tldr fields for PROS-003 and PROS-007 (analyst can provide these if requested), (2) consider closing ISS-677 as Mode 5 is now operational and first audit complete, (3) next audit in 7 days unless poller detects PROS section changes.
